title: Today's Tech Brief: YouTube's AI Feed and Google I/O Insights
summary: YouTube's new AI-powered video feed, Google I/O's AI-driven science shift, and more
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for YouTube will let you ask AI to make a custom video feed
imageCaption: YouTube will let you ask AI to make a custom video feed (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. I've got some interesting updates for you today. YouTube is taking a step into the AI-driven world by letting users ask for custom video feeds. Imagine telling a platform what kind of videos you're in the mood for - workout guides, hobby inspiration, or maybe some cooking shows - and it serves them up. That's what YouTube is experimenting with. I mean, who hasn't spent hours scrolling through their feeds, trying to find something that catches their eye? This could change the game.

According to The Verge, YouTube's new feature allows users to describe the kind of videos they want to see, and an AI will curate a feed based on that input. This could change how we interact with video content platforms. It's like having a personal video assistant, without the need for complicated prompts or queries. I'm curious to see how this will affect the way we discover new content and channels.

YouTube will let you ask AI to make a custom video feed, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Streaming AI News YouTube will let you ask AI to make a custom video feed Just describe the kind of videos you want to see, from workout guides to hobby inspiration. Just describe the kind of videos you want to see, from workout guides to hobby inspiration. by Jess Weatherbed May 28, 2026, 8:49 AM UTC You can enter your own prompt, or select from the sugges... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Google I/O showed how the path for AI-driven science is shifting is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY During Tuesday’s Google I/O keynote, Demis Hassabis, the CEO of Google DeepMind, proclaimed that we are currently “standing in the foothills of the singularity.” It was a striking statement—the singularity is the theoretical future moment when AI rapidly exceeds human intelligence and dramatically transforms the world. But what struck me a... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
